title: HSE Guidance: Risk Assessment — A Brief Guide to Controlling Risks in the Workplace
chapters: HSE guidance INDG163 · Covers the 5-step risk assessment process · Made under MHSWR 1999 reg.3 · Applies to all employers with employees
desc: The HSE risk assessment guidance sets out the five-step process for carrying out workplace risk assessments as required by the Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 regulation 3. Employers with 5 or more employees must record the significant findings. The five steps are: (1) identify the hazards; (2) decide who might be harmed and how; (3) evaluate the risks and decide on precautions; (4) record your significant findings; (5) review your risk assessment and update if necessary. Risk assessment is the foundation of the UK health and safety regulatory framework — it drives all subsequent control measures, training, monitoring, and review. Special assessments are required for new and expectant mothers (MHSWR reg.16), young persons (MHSWR reg.19), and for specific hazards including manual handling, DSE, COSHH, noise, vibration, and fire.
legalStatus: HSE guidance. The legal obligation to carry out risk assessment is in MHSWR 1999 reg.3. The HSE guidance provides the practical methodology.

sections.title: Identify hazards, assess risks, implement controls, record, review
sections.summary: Step 1 — Identify the hazards: walk around the workplace, consider what could cause harm. Consult employees. Check manufacturers' instructions. Review accident and ill-health records. Consider long-term health hazards (noise, harmful substances) as well as safety hazards (slips, trips, falls, working at height, machinery). Step 2 — Decide who might be harmed and how: employees, agency workers, contractors, visitors, members of the public, lone workers, homeworkers, people with disabilities, new and expectant mothers, young workers. Step 3 — Evaluate the risks and decide on precautions: for each hazard, decide whether existing precautions are adequate or if more needs to be done. Apply the hierarchy of controls: eliminate the hazard, substitute with something less hazardous, use engineering controls, use administrative controls, use personal protective equipment (PPE) as last resort. Step 4 — Record your significant findings: employers with 5+ employees must record the significant findings of the risk assessment and any group of employees identified as being especially at risk. Step 5 — Review your risk assessment: risk assessments are not one-off exercises. Review when circumstances change, when there has been an accident or near miss, or when there is new information about risks.
sections.text:
The five steps to risk assessment:

Step 1: Identify the hazards
• Walk around the workplace and look at what could reasonably be expected to cause harm
• Ask employees — they may have noticed things that are not immediately obvious
• Check manufacturers' instructions and data sheets for chemicals and equipment
• Review accident and ill-health records
• Consider long-term health hazards (e.g. high noise levels, exposure to harmful substances)

Step 2: Decide who might be harmed and how
• Identify groups who may be particularly at risk: young workers, new and expectant mothers, people with disabilities, lone workers, homeworkers, agency workers, contractors, visitors, members of the public

Step 3: Evaluate the risks and decide on precautions
• Compare what you are already doing with good practice — check HSE guidance for your industry
• Apply the hierarchy of controls: (1) eliminate; (2) substitute; (3) engineering controls; (4) administrative controls; (5) PPE
• Consider whether you can get rid of the hazard altogether

Step 4: Record your significant findings
• Employers with 5 or more employees must record the significant findings
• Record what the hazards are, who might be harmed, and what you are doing to control the risks

Step 5: Review your risk assessment and update if necessary
• Review when there has been a significant change (new equipment, substances, procedures)
• Review after an accident, near miss, or case of ill health
• Review when new information about risks becomes available
• Review at regular intervals as appropriate

versions.description: MHSWR 1999 reg.3 (in force from 29 December 1999) imposed the legal obligation to carry out suitable and sufficient risk assessment. The HSE 5-step guidance (INDG163) was first published in 1999.
HSE INDG163 revised and updated. Additional guidance on assessing risks from specific hazards (manual handling, DSE, COSHH, stress).

leadingCases.name: R v Porter [2008] EWCA Crim 1271
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal considered the adequacy of a risk assessment and held that a suitable and sufficient risk assessment must be specific to the work activity, identify all significant hazards, assess the level of risk, identify existing controls, and determine whether further controls are needed. A generic risk assessment that does not address the specific hazards of the activity is insufficient.
leadingCases.significance: Established that risk assessments must be specific to the actual work activity — generic assessments are insufficient

leadingCases.name: R v Tangerine Confectionery Ltd [2011] EWCA Crim 2015
leadingCases.heldText: The Court of Appeal confirmed that the burden of proving reasonable practicability rests on the employer. A risk assessment is the primary evidence of what the employer knew about the risks and what they did to address them. An absent or inadequate risk assessment undermines the employer's defence.
leadingCases.significance: Risk assessment as the primary evidence of employer compliance — absence undermines the defence of reasonable practicability.

leadingCases.name: Kennedy v Cordia (Services) LLP [2016] UKSC 6
leadingCases.heldText: The Supreme Court held that the employer's risk assessment was inadequate because it failed to identify the specific risk of ice on external paths. The court confirmed that a risk assessment must be specific to the actual risks faced by employees and that a failure to carry out an adequate assessment is strong evidence of a breach of the employer's common law duty of care.
leadingCases.significance: Supreme Court authority — inadequate risk assessment is strong evidence of breach of duty. Risk assessment must address specific hazards, not just generic categories.

refs: MHSWR 1999 reg.3 (legal obligation to carry out risk assessment)
HSWA 1974 s.2 (employer's general duty — risk assessment evidences compliance)
MHSWR 1999 reg.16 (risk assessment for new and expectant mothers)
MHSWR 1999 reg.19 (risk assessment for young persons)
HSE INDG163 (A Brief Guide to Controlling Risks in the Workplace)

pendingAmendments: HSE risk assessment guidance periodically updated — monitoring

parliamentaryOrigin.enablingAct: Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 s.15 (regulations power) and Management of Health and Safety at Work Regulations 1999 reg.3
parliamentaryOrigin.euDirective: Council Directive 89/391/EEC (Framework Directive on Safety and Health at Work) Art.6(3)(a)
parliamentaryOrigin.note: The legal obligation to carry out risk assessment derives from MHSWR 1999 reg.3, made under HSWA 1974. The EU Framework Directive required member states to impose a risk assessment obligation. HSE INDG163 is guidance, not legally binding — the Regulations are the legal obligation.
